name: Mozzarella Cheese
recipeIngredient: Rennet (1/2 Hansens tablet) dissolved in about half a cup of cold water
recipeInstructions:
1. Usually makes 4 half-pound cheeses.
2. Mozzarella is one of several kinds of "plastic-curd" cheeses, originating in Italy. In making them the curds are kneaded, which expels whey and produces plasticity.
3. Because of their dense texture they keep well in warm climates and are ideal for smoking.
4. Provolone is an aged version of a plastic-curd cheese.
5. Mozzarella is one of the most versatile cheeses to make at home, since it tastes wonderful freshly made, freezes well, and can be used like an aged cheese in cooking, melting readily when heated.
1. Start this cheese in the evening. Maintain the milk at 90°F in a double boiler.
2. Mix the buttermilk and yogurt separately with a little of the milk to remove lumps, then blend into the rest of the milk.
3. Add the rennet solution and mix thoroughly.
4. Let sit until the curd sets and breaks clearly when tested with a finger - about 20 to 30 minutes.
5. Cut the curd into 1/2-inch cubes as evenly as possible.
6. Maintain at 90°F for 15 minutes, stirring gently with a clean hand (the curds are fragile because they have not been cooked).
7. Gently pour the curds into a cloth-lined colander. When the whey has drained, the curd should be in one solid piece.
8. Rinse in cold water, then soak in a pan of cold water for 15 minutes. If it is a large batch, cut the curd into several blocks, 4 or 5 inches square.
9. Drain off most of the water, then refrigerate the curds, or keep in a cool, 40°F place (leave them in a colander or other container that allows drainage).
10. The next day, warm the cheese to room temperature so it will ripen, or become more acidic.
11. Test the cheese for acidity: Cut off a small piece of cheese and cut it into three 1/2-inch cubes. Heat several cups of water in a saucepan to 165°F. Put in the cubes and stir for 5 minutes. Remove the cubes and mold them together like modeling clay. Reheat the lump of cheese in the water for a minute, then remove and work or mold it together a little more.
12. After repeating several times, try to pull the curd apart. If it pulls into a long rope and can be molded together again, it is ready. It will have a glossy surface and cloud the water only slightly.
13. Cut the cheese into small cubes and put them in a pan. Heat water to 170°F and pour enough over them to cover the curds by about 2 inches. Keep a thermometer in the pan and let the temperature drop to 135°F.
14. Press the cubes together, then knead the cheese by stretching and pulling it, as if working modeling clay. It should become "plastic" and stretch into long strands.
15. When it does, shape into half-pound balls or make a thick rope, fold it in half, and twist several times to make a decorative oblong cheese.
16. Mozzarella can be dipped in hot water to make a glossy surface, or wrapped in cheese cloth to protect it.
17. To keep the cheese very fresh tasting for up to a week, keep it in a bowl of water in the refrigerator and change the water every day.
18. To salt mozzarella for longer keeping or to prepare it for smoking, soak it in brine for 4 or 5 hours (see Brined Cheese).
19. Smoked Mozzarella: Mozzarella and other firm cheeses can be cold smoked for flavor. Salting and smoking both help preserve the cheese by drying it and discouraging bacteria and insects. Set the cheese on a rack in the smoker, or wrap in cheesecloth and hang it. Keep the temperature below 90°F to prevent sweating off butterfat or melting. Smoke at 60 to 85°F for 4 to 15 hours.
20. Brined Cheese: Press the cheese for 5 or 6 hours, or overnight without salting it. Make enough brine to cover the cheese by about an inch using 4-1/2 tablespoons of plain salt for every quart of water needed. Soak the cheese from 12 to 24 hours (a small cheese requires less time than a large one). Turn the cheese once or twice to ensure that the brine penetrates all sides. Drain for about an hour on a cloth-covered rack. Cover and refrigerate. Keeps for a week or more, longer than most fresh cheeses.
